figures.gloss: Gaulish 'Thunderer', wheel in hand — his name is simply the Celtic word for thunder.
Shape-shifting crow-goddess of battle, fate and death, who decides who falls and washes their bloodied arms at the ford.
Smith of the Túatha Dé Danann whose weapons never miss, and whose ale grants the gods their deathlessness.
Ancestral mother of the Túatha Dé Danann — the very 'people of the goddess Danu'.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rigid, frontal sky-father wielding absolute atmospheric power through symbolic implements of symmetry and rotation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fearsome female ritual authorities steeped in the imagery of death, warfare, and the collection of the slain.
Both are feminine powers of fate and death, allotting the doom of warriors — Manat measures the appointed term as the Morrigan presides over slaughter; the goddess as apportioner of mortality.
Wheel-and-thunder gods of the western and eastern IE world; both carry the *(s)tenh2- / *Perkwunos thunder-complex, no proven common name.
Both touch the allotment of life and death, but Laima is a benevolent midwife of fate where the Morrigan is its war-shadow — a loose, divergent parallel.
A sovereign female power of the land tied to weather, omen and the punishment of oath-breakers — independent witnesses to the moral-sovereign goddess.
Both feed the late hagiographic dragon-slaying frame (Michael, the saint vs. the serpent); offered as structural parallel only, heavily Christian-mediated.

Speculative: a fierce battle-mother who scatters the enemies of her people — Sidpa Gyalmo and the Morrígan-figure as the war-band aspect of the great-mother; a loose cross-family rhyme of the martial feminine, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mythic smith whose metallurgical mastery equips the divine hosts, dwelling in an otherworldly domain.

The battlefield goddess who feeds on the slain and grants or withholds victory — Koṟṟavai and the Morrígan as the war-mother who haunts the field of the dead, a striking structural parallel across wholly separate families.

The carrion-spirits of the battlefield, feeding on the slain where the war-goddess reigns — the pēy and the Morrígan's crows as the appetite of the field of the dead, a parallel of the same grim image.
Both are winged female death-attendants who mark rather than cause the appointed end — the Morrigan over the battlefield, Vanth at the deathbed.
Powerful, fearsome female sovereigns set against the heroes; Louhi the northern sorceress-queen and the Morrigan share menace and command, but the parallel is loose and the families unrelated.
Tutelary keepers of the wild presiding over a sacred natural order; Mielikki of the forest loosely parallels the great mother-of-the-land figures — a soft, divergent rhyme rather than a firm joint.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are Indo-European thunderers associated with a specific symbolic weapon (hammer and wheel) and invoked for the protection of the tribe.

GOLD SEAM: Both are smiths of the divine — Goibniu is the Celtic smith-god who forges weapons and brews the ale of immortality, while the Hermetic alchemist forges the Philosopher's Stone that grants eternal life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody the sacred, life-giving flow of natural waters and springs in ancient European tribal religion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A divine smith whose forge is the center of mythical creation and sustenance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ancient Indo-European archetype of the divine river mother anchoring the fertility and sovereignty of the land.

Thunder as a venerated power, but Pillán diverges sharply: where Taranis is a sky-borne thunder-god, the Mapuche thunder IS the ancestral dead speaking from the volcano — the storm-wielder joint reached through genealogy, not a separate deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Druidry directly imports and invokes the Celtic figure of Danu, elevating her as the supreme Earth Mother archetype in contemporary liturgy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, weeping female specter who appears as a direct, unalterable omen of impending death.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as divine smiths and martial forces whose mastery over metal clears the path for civilization and war.
GOLD SEAM: Implacable, terrifying sovereign deities linked to wheels, fire, and the reception of ultimate human sacrifices in classical historiography.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Terrifying, blood-soaked goddesses of war and destruction who laugh amidst carnage and revel in the dissolution of enemies.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient European thundering sky god, wielding the terrifying acoustic and destructive power of the storm.
GOLD SEAM: The Crone aspect echoes the dark, prophetic, and death-bringing nature of the Celtic Badb.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion of the Celtic-speaking peoples of Iron Age Europe — Gaul, Britain, Ireland, Iberia, the Alpine lands, and Galatia — from roughly the Hallstatt/La Tène horizon through Romanization and Christianization. No insider account survives: the religion must be triangulated from archaeology, Greco-Roman testimony, Romano-Celtic inscriptions, and medieval Irish and Welsh literature written down centuries later by Christians.
dossier.cosmos: No unified cosmology is attested. The evidence points to sanctity concentrated in the land itself — springs, rivers, groves (nemeton), and waters receiving deliberate deposits — and, in the Insular literature, an Otherworld adjacent to this one, entered through burial mounds (síd), water, or mist. Classical authors report druidic teaching that souls do not perish but pass into other bodies (Caesar, Diodorus), a doctrine they compared to Pythagorean transmigration.
dossier.practice: In Gaul and Britain a professional priestly-juridical class, the druids, taught orally, judged disputes, and presided over sacrifice; classical sources allege human sacrifice (with evident polemical interest), and finds such as the Lindow Man bog body are argued both ways. Votive deposition — weapons, cauldrons, and metalwork sunk in water — is the best-attested ritual act archaeologically. Early Irish literature preserves the quarter-day festivals Samain, Imbolc, Beltaine, and Lugnasad, along with sacral-kingship rites; how far these generalize to the ancient Continent is uncertain.
dossier.canon: None native — the druids declined to commit their teaching to writing (Caesar, Gallic War 6.14). The sources are external: Posidonian ethnography via Caesar, Strabo, and Diodorus; epigraphy and iconography (the Coligny calendar, dedications to Taranis, Epona, Lugus); and the Christian-era Irish sagas (Táin Bó Cúailnge, Lebor Gabála Érenn) and the Welsh Mabinogi.
dossier.reflects: Sanctity located in landscape rather than text — and a standing demonstration of how little of a purely oral religion survives except through the accounts of outsiders and successors.
sources: Miranda Green, 'The Gods of the Celts' (1986)
Ronald Hutton, 'Blood and Mistletoe: The History of the Druids in Britain' (2009)
